findings.title: profile parameter declared in BuildUserMessageParams but never used inside buildUserMessage
findings.description: BuildUserMessageParams declares a `profile: HealthProfile` field (line 1688), and the function signature accepts it via the params object, but buildUserMessage never destructures or references `profile` anywhere in its body. This is dead interface surface that misleads callers into thinking the profile shapes the user message payload, but it does not. It also creates a TypeScript type contract the implementation doesn't honour.
findings.fix: Either remove `profile` from BuildUserMessageParams entirely (callers pass it but the value is discarded), or identify the intended use (possibly to gate reproductive health inclusion) and wire it up. If the intent was to pass profile to buildPartialDayContext, that function does not accept profile either — evaluate whether profile is needed downstream.

findings.title: Stale comment references internal sprint terminology ('Wave 4D') that no longer maps to any known milestone
findings.description: `newFoodsThisWeek` is hardcoded to `[]` with a comment saying it will be wired in 'Wave 4D when the compiler moves server-side.' The sprint wave terminology used here is from a previous planning model that has been superseded. The feature is silently broken: the AI payload always receives an empty array for new foods this week, but the system prompt references it as a meaningful data point. The comment gives no actionable resolution path and will confuse future readers.
findings.fix: Replace the wave-specific comment with a proper TODO that references the architectural constraint (need server-side context / Convex query to resolve newFoods from knownFoods). Consider either removing the field from the payload entirely (so the AI is not misled by an always-empty array) or implementing it properly. An always-empty field sent to the AI is noise.

findings.title: Unsafe type assertion on sanitized AI input in fetchWeeklySummary
findings.description: `sanitizeUnknownStringsDeep` returns `unknown`, and the result is immediately asserted with `as WeeklySummaryInput` (line 2388). This is a silent type lie: if sanitization modifies the structure in a way that breaks the WeeklySummaryInput shape, TypeScript will not catch it. The sanitized value is then serialised directly into an AI prompt payload without further validation.
findings.fix: Add a runtime shape check after sanitization, or define a `toWeeklySummaryInput(raw: unknown): WeeklySummaryInput` parser that validates the required fields (weekOf, conversationMessages, suggestions, bowelNotes) before the assertion. This is consistent with the defensive approach used throughout the rest of the file (e.g., parseAiInsight).

findings.title: Stale reference to deleted reproductive health feature remains in system prompt
findings.description: The system prompt (line 1232) still contains the instruction: 'If reproductive/cycle data is provided (menstrual cycle phase, bleeding, pregnancy, perimenopause/menopause, hormonal contraception/HRT), treat it as OPTIONAL context that can modify motility...' This is a survivor from the deleted reproductive health feature. The backend no longer includes reproductive log data in the AI payload — `reproductiveLogs` was removed from `RecentEventsResult` and no reproductive data is passed to the AI. This instruction is therefore dead prose in the prompt, wasting tokens and potentially confusing the model.
findings.fix: Remove the reproductive/cycle modifier paragraph from the system prompt in `buildSystemPrompt`. The model will never receive this data, so the instruction serves no purpose and inflates token cost on every request.

findings.title: System prompt uses 'didYouKnow' field name but the JSON schema uses 'educationalInsight'
findings.description: The length directive strings (lines 1031, 1041, 1049) reference a field called `didYouKnow` ('- didYouKnow: 1 sentence max...'). The actual JSON schema exposed to the model (line 1484) and enforced by `parseAiInsight` uses `educationalInsight`. This creates a field-name mismatch in the model's instructions. The model could attempt to write a `didYouKnow` key that is ignored by the parser, resulting in silently dropped educational content.
findings.fix: Replace all three occurrences of `didYouKnow` in the length directive strings with `educationalInsight` to match the schema the model is instructed to emit.

findings.title: timeSinceLastBowelMovement always outputs '\{n\} hours' including '1 hours' — grammatically broken
findings.description: `buildPartialDayContext` always formats the string as `${hoursSinceLastBm} hours` (line 1666). When the rounded value is 1, the output is '1 hours'. This string is injected into the AI prompt and may also surface in the UI via partialDayContext. The test suite only asserts '5 hours', '2 hours', and '3 hours' — the 1-hour case is never tested.
findings.fix: Add a singular/plural branch: `` hoursSinceLastBm === 1 ? '1 hour' : `${hoursSinceLastBm} hours` ``. Add a corresponding test case for the 1-hour edge case in the test file.

findings.title: Habit streak algorithm has an off-by-one in the consecutive-day index calculation
findings.description: In `computeHabitStreaks`, the expected day for index `i` is calculated as: `new Date(new Date(lastDay).getTime() - (sortedDays.length - 1 - i) * MS_PER_DAY)` (lines 698-700). This computes the expected date relative to `lastDay` as if all N days were a consecutive block of exactly `sortedDays.length - 1 - i` days before `lastDay`. However, this is a fixed-offset formula — it does not actually verify consecutive calendar-day adjacency during the backward walk. It works correctly only when there are no gaps at all before the current position. For example, if `sortedDays` = ['2026-03-25', '2026-03-28', '2026-03-29'] and `lastDay` = '2026-03-29', the expected day for index 0 is `2026-03-29 - 2 days = 2026-03-27`, but `sortedDays[0]` is `2026-03-25`. The break fires correctly in this case. However, if gaps exist mid-sequence but the first element happens to match the computed offset, the algorithm could overcount. The logic should instead compare each adjacent pair of days directly during the backward walk.
findings.fix: Replace the index-offset formula with a direct adjacent-day check: compare `sortedDays[i]` to `sortedDays[i+1]` to verify they are exactly 1 day apart. Walk backwards from the last element, incrementing the streak count for each consecutive adjacent pair.

findings.title: 'caps at 96h' test retains pregnancyStatus: 'pregnant' from the deleted reproductive modifier — the test is now misleading
findings.description: The 'caps at 96h' test at line 654 sets `pregnancyStatus: 'pregnant'` in the profile to imply it was contributing to the modifier. But the pregnancy modifier was deleted from `getFoodWindowHours` in this PR. The test still passes (72 + 24 = 96 from the opioid+iron combo), but the pregnancy field now does nothing. A developer reading this test will reasonably assume pregnancy is what caps at 96h, which is incorrect after the refactor.
findings.fix: Remove the `reproductiveHealth` spread from this test. The capping at 96h is fully explained by `medications: 'opioid pain medication, iron supplements'` — both are subsumed by the single `hasSlowTransitModifier` boolean. The test description should also be updated to reflect the actual scenario: 'caps at 96h when opioid + iron present (single boolean modifier + 72h base)'.

findings.title: MINIMAL_PROFILE in buildUserMessage tests retains a full reproductiveHealth block that is never exercised and suggests the feature is still active
findings.description: The `MINIMAL_PROFILE` constant at line 402 includes a complete `reproductiveHealth` block with 17 fields (lines 434-458). Similarly, the secondary `BASE_PROFILE` at line 795 also carries the full block (lines 826-849). The reproductive health feature was stripped in this PR. These large fixture blocks now serve only as required type-completeness fillers (since `HealthProfile.reproductiveHealth` is still required in `domain.ts`). They add visual noise, make the profile fixtures hard to scan, and may give the false impression that reproductive health data flows through to the AI.
findings.fix: This is blocked on removing `reproductiveHealth` from the `HealthProfile` type in `domain.ts` (which is a larger schema change). In the meantime, add a comment on the `reproductiveHealth` block in both fixtures making it explicit that this field is present only to satisfy the TypeScript shape contract and has no runtime effect in the AI pipeline. Longer-term: remove the field from the domain type entirely.

findings.title: stillInTransit test uses learnedTransitCenterMinutes of 90 * 60 minutes (= 5400 minutes = 90 hours) — value is in wrong unit
findings.description: The test at line 1526 sets `learnedTransitCenterMinutes: 90 * 60`. The `*` here is 90 hours multiplied by 60, giving 5400 minutes — but the field name ends in `Minutes`, meaning 5400 minutes = 90 hours. This is technically correct for a 90-hour transit time but the expression `90 * 60` is the wrong mental model: it looks like someone converted hours to minutes, but the field already expects minutes. For an actual 90-hour transit, the correct value is `90 * 60` minutes, so the result is right. However, the comment says '90 hours center', and the production code interprets the field in minutes, so for a realistic transit time this is fine. But the unit confusion is real: if a developer reads `90 * 60` they may think the center is 90 minutes (not 90 hours). The expression should be written explicitly to avoid misreading.
findings.fix: Clarify the expression. If the intent is 90-hour transit (which is very long and clinically implausible for a human), write `90 * 60 /* = 90 hours in minutes */`. More importantly, consider whether 90-hour transit is a realistic test fixture — a 4-hour transit scenario would be `240` (4 * 60 minutes), which is a more clinically reasonable test value.

findings.title: Test 'newFoodsThisWeek returns empty array (placeholder)' tests a known-broken placeholder, not behaviour
findings.description: The test at line 1427 asserts that `buildDeltaSignals` returns `newFoodsThisWeek: []`. This is testing the placeholder stub (line 729 in the implementation), not any real behaviour. The test will continue to pass even if the feature is accidentally removed entirely. This is a low-value test that pins a broken default rather than the intended behaviour.
findings.fix: Either remove the test (it adds no safety value) or replace it with a skip/todo marker (`it.todo('newFoodsThisWeek: requires server-side context — wire when moved to Convex action')`) so future developers understand why it is not implemented.

findings.title: No tests for parseAiInsight's foodAssessments parsing, clinicalReasoning, or the enforceNovelEducationalInsight deduplication logic
findings.description: `parseAiInsight` has a moderately complex filter for `foodAssessments` (lines 1868-1889 in the implementation) requiring all six fields to be present and valid. There are zero test cases for this validator. Similarly, `clinicalReasoning` parsing has no dedicated test (though it appears in FULL_VALID_RESPONSE, it is never specifically asserted). The `enforceNovelEducationalInsight` / fallback bank logic is completely untested. These are paths that directly affect the quality of AI outputs shown to a post-surgical patient.
findings.fix: Add test cases for: (a) `foodAssessments` with valid and malformed entries (missing verdict, invalid causalRole, etc.), (b) `clinicalReasoning` null fallback, and (c) `enforceNovelEducationalInsight` selecting a fallback when the AI returns a duplicate educational insight.

findings.title: sanitizeNameForPrompt strips tags but does not guard against Unicode bidirectional override characters or prompt control sequences
findings.description: `sanitizeNameForPrompt` removes `<tag>` patterns and truncates to 50 characters (lines 56-58). This guards against basic XML injection. However, it does not strip Unicode bidirectional control characters (U+202E RIGHT-TO-LEFT OVERRIDE, U+2066-U+2069 DIRECTIONAL ISOLATES, etc.) which could confuse some LLM tokenizers or display renderers. It also does not strip null bytes or other control characters. Given the name is wrapped in `<patient_name>` XML delimiters in the prompt (line 1183), the risk is limited — but the guard is not complete.
findings.fix: Add a step to strip non-printable Unicode categories after the XML-tag stripping: `stripped.replace(/[\p{C}\p{Cf}]/gu, '').trim()`. This removes control characters and format characters (including BiDi overrides) while preserving normal Unicode names.
